1→# Dependency Injection with Hilt
     2→
     3→Set up Hilt as the DI framework across the application. Configure Application-scoped, Activity-scoped, and ViewModel-scoped components. Provide singleton instances for database, networking, scanner manager, and repository classes.
     4→
     5→## Rationale
     6→Hilt is specified in the tech stack (CLAUDE.md) and is essential for testability, modular architecture, and proper singleton lifecycle management. The NewlandScannerManager must be Application-scoped, and repositories need proper scoping for data layer isolation.
     7→
     8→## User Stories
     9→- As a developer, I want dependency injection so that components are loosely coupled and easily testable
    10→
    11→## Acceptance Criteria
    12→- [ ] Hilt configured with @HiltAndroidApp Application class
    13→- [ ] At least one @Module providing Application-scoped dependencies
    14→- [ ] ViewModels use @HiltViewModel annotation
    15→- [ ] Activities annotated with @AndroidEntryPoint
    16→- [ ] Dependency graph compiles without errors
    17→

1→package com.inventory.di
     2→
     3→import android.content.Context
     4→import com.inventory.scanner.NewlandScannerManager
     5→import dagger.Module
     6→import dagger.Provides
     7→import dagger.hilt.InstallIn
     8→import dagger.hilt.android.qualifiers.ApplicationContext
     9→import dagger.hilt.components.SingletonComponent
    10→import javax.inject.Singleton
    11→
    12→/**
    13→ * Hilt dependency injection module for scanner-related dependencies.
    14→ *
    15→ * This module provides singleton instances of scanner components to be injected
    16→ * throughout the application. All providers are scoped to SingletonComponent,
    17→ * meaning they live for the entire application lifetime.
    18→ */
    19→@Module
    20→@InstallIn(SingletonComponent::class)
    21→object ScannerModule {
    22→
    23→    /**
    24→     * Provides the singleton instance of NewlandScannerManager.
    25→     *
    26→     * The scanner manager handles lifecycle management of the MT90 barcode scanner,
    27→     * including BroadcastReceiver registration, scan event processing, and reactive
    28→     * event emission via SharedFlow.
    29→     *
    30→     * @param context Application context for BroadcastReceiver registration
    31→     * @return Singleton NewlandScannerManager instance
    32→     */
    33→    @Provides
    34→    @Singleton
    35→    fun provideNewlandScannerManager(
    36→        @ApplicationContext context: Context
    37→    ): NewlandScannerManager {
    38→        return NewlandScannerManager(context)
    39→    }
    40→}
    41→

1→package com.inventory.scanner
     2→
     3→import android.content.BroadcastReceiver
     4→import android.content.Context
     5→import android.content.Intent
     6→import android.util.Log
     7→import android.view.KeyEvent
     8→import dagger.hilt.android.qualifiers.ApplicationContext
     9→import kotlinx.coroutines.CoroutineScope
    10→import kotlinx.coroutines.Dispatchers
    11→import kotlinx.coroutines.SupervisorJob
    12→import kotlinx.coroutines.flow.MutableSharedFlow
    13→import kotlinx.coroutines.flow.SharedFlow
    14→import kotlinx.coroutines.flow.asSharedFlow
    15→import kotlinx.coroutines.launch
    16→import javax.inject.Inject
    17→import javax.inject.Singleton
    18→
    19→/**
    20→ * Singleton manager for the Newland MT90 barcode scanner.
    21→ *
    22→ * Handles lifecycle management of the scanner BroadcastReceiver, processes scan events,
    23→ * implements debouncing to prevent duplicate scans, and provides a reactive SharedFlow
    24→ * for consuming scan results in ViewModels and UI layers.
    25→ *
    26→ * Key features:
    27→ * - Registers/unregisters BroadcastReceiver for nlscan.action.SCANNER_RESULT
    28→ * - Handles hardware scan button (F6 key)
    29→ * - Supports programmatic scan triggering via nlscan.action.SCANNER_TRIG
    30→ * - 300ms debounce protection against double-scans
    31→ * - Reactive API via SharedFlow<ScanResult>
    32→ *
    33→ * @property context Application context injected by Hilt
    34→ */
    35→@Singleton
    36→class NewlandScannerManager @Inject constructor(
    37→    @ApplicationContext private val context: Context
    38→) {
    39→    companion object {
    40→        private const val TAG = "NewlandScannerManager"
    41→
    42→        // Newland MT90 scanner broadcast actions
    43→        private const val ACTION_SCANNER_RESULT = "nlscan.action.SCANNER_RESULT"
    44→        private const val ACTION_SCANNER_TRIG = "nlscan.action.SCANNER_TRIG"
    45→
    46→        // Intent extra keys for scan data (based on Newland MT90 SDK)
    47→        private const val EXTRA_BARCODE_DATA = "SCAN_BARCODE1"
    48→        private const val EXTRA_BARCODE_TYPE = "SCAN_BARCODE_TYPE"
    49→
    50→        // Debounce protection: ignore scans within 300ms of the previous scan
    51→        private const val DEBOUNCE_DELAY_MS = 300L
    52→    }
    53→
    54→    /**
    55→     * Tracks whether the BroadcastReceiver is currently registered.
    56→     * Prevents double registration/unregistration.
    57→     */
    58→    private var isRegistered = false
    59→
    60→    /**
    61→     * Timestamp of the last processed scan in milliseconds (System.currentTimeMillis()).
    62→     * Used for debounce protection to prevent duplicate scans within 300ms.
    63→     */
    64→    private var lastScanTimestamp = 0L
    65→
    66→    /**
    67→     * CoroutineScope for emitting scan events to SharedFlow.
    68→     * Uses SupervisorJob to isolate failures and Dispatchers.Main for UI thread safety.
    69→     */
    70→    private val scannerScope = CoroutineScope(SupervisorJob() + Dispatchers.Main.immediate)
    71→
    72→    /**
    73→     * Internal MutableSharedFlow for emitting scan results.
    74→     * Configured with replay = 0 (no replay) and extraBufferCapacity = 1 to buffer
    75→     * one event if no collectors are actively listening.
    76→     */
    77→    private val _scanEvents = MutableSharedFlow<ScanResult>(
    78→        replay = 0,
    79→        extraBufferCapacity = 1
    80→    )
    81→
    82→    /**
    83→     * Public SharedFlow for consuming scan results in ViewModels and UI layers.
    84→     *
    85→     * Collectors will receive ScanResult events emitted when the scanner captures
    86→     * a barcode. Use this in ViewModels with viewModelScope.launch { scanEvents.collect {...} }
    87→     *
    88→     * Example usage:
    89→     * ```
    90→     * viewModelScope.launch {
    91→     *     scannerManager.scanEvents.collect { scanResult ->
    92→     *         Log.d("MyViewModel", "Scanned: ${scanResult.barcode}")
    93→     *     }
    94→     * }
    95→     * ```
    96→     */
    97→    val scanEvents: SharedFlow<ScanResult> = _scanEvents.asSharedFlow()
    98→
    99→    /**
   100→     * BroadcastReceiver for handling scan results from the Newland MT90 scanner.
   101→     *
   102→     * Listens for nlscan.action.SCANNER_RESULT broadcasts and extracts barcode data
   103→     * and barcode type from the intent extras.
   104→     */
   105→    private val scanReceiver = object : BroadcastReceiver() {
   106→        override fun onReceive(context: Context?, intent: Intent?) {
   107→            if (intent?.action != ACTION_SCANNER_RESULT) {
   108→                return
   109→            }
   110→
   111→            val barcode = intent.getStringExtra(EXTRA_BARCODE_DATA)
   112→            val barcodeType = intent.getStringExtra(EXTRA_BARCODE_TYPE)
   113→
   114→            if (barcode.isNullOrBlank()) {
   115→                Log.w(TAG, "Received scan result with empty barcode")
   116→                return
   117→            }
   118→
   119→            val scanResult = ScanResult(
   120→                barcode = barcode,
   121→                barcodeType = barcodeType ?: "UNKNOWN"
   122→            )
   123→
   124→            Log.d(TAG, "Scan received: ${scanResult.barcode} (${scanResult.barcodeType})")
   125→
   126→            // Debounce protection: ignore scans within 300ms of the previous scan
   127→            val currentTime = System.currentTimeMillis()
   128→            val timeSinceLastScan = currentTime - lastScanTimestamp
   129→
   130→            if (timeSinceLastScan < DEBOUNCE_DELAY_MS) {
   131→                Log.d(TAG, "Scan ignored (debounce): ${timeSinceLastScan}ms since last scan")
   132→                return
   133→            }
   134→
   135→            // Update timestamp before emitting to prevent race conditions
   136→            lastScanTimestamp = currentTime
   137→
   138→            // Emit scan result to SharedFlow for reactive consumption
   139→            scannerScope.launch {
   140→                _scanEvents.emit(scanResult)
   141→                Log.d(TAG, "Scan event emitted to SharedFlow")
   142→            }
   143→        }
   144→    }
   145→
   146→    /**
   147→     * Registers the BroadcastReceiver to listen for scanner broadcasts.
   148→     *
   149→     * This should be called when the Activity/Fragment enters the foreground
   150→     * (typically in onResume or onStart) to begin receiving scan events.
   151→     *
   152→     * Safe to call multiple times - will not double-register.
   153→     */
   154→    fun register() {
   155→        if (isRegistered) {
   156→            Log.d(TAG, "Scanner receiver already registered, skipping")
   157→            return
   158→        }
   159→
   160→        val filter = android.content.IntentFilter(ACTION_SCANNER_RESULT)
   161→        context.registerReceiver(scanReceiver, filter)
   162→        isRegistered = true
   163→        Log.d(TAG, "Scanner receiver registered")
   164→    }
   165→
   166→    /**
   167→     * Unregisters the BroadcastReceiver to stop listening for scanner broadcasts.
   168→     *
   169→     * This MUST be called when the Activity/Fragment is destroyed (typically in
   170→     * onPause or onStop) to prevent memory leaks.
   171→     *
   172→     * Safe to call multiple times - will not crash if already unregistered.
   173→     */
   174→    fun unregister() {
   175→        if (!isRegistered) {
   176→            Log.d(TAG, "Scanner receiver not registered, skipping unregister")
   177→            return
   178→        }
   179→
   180→        context.unregisterReceiver(scanReceiver)
   181→        isRegistered = false
   182→        Log.d(TAG, "Scanner receiver unregistered")
   183→    }
   184→
   185→    /**
   186→     * Handles hardware key events from the Newland MT90 scanner.
   187→     *
   188→     * The MT90's hardware scan button generates a KeyEvent.KEYCODE_F6 event.
   189→     * When detected, this method triggers a scan by sending the nlscan.action.SCANNER_TRIG
   190→     * broadcast intent.
   191→     *
   192→     * This method should be called from your Activity's onKeyDown override:
   193→     * ```
   194→     * override fun onKeyDown(keyCode: Int, event: KeyEvent): Boolean {
   195→     *     if (scannerManager.onKeyDown(keyCode, event)) {
   196→     *         return true
   197→     *     }
   198→     *     return super.onKeyDown(keyCode, event)
   199→     * }
   200→     * ```
   201→     *
   202→     * @param keyCode The key code from the KeyEvent
   203→     * @param event The KeyEvent object
   204→     * @return true if the key event was handled (F6 key pressed), false otherwise
   205→     */
   206→    fun onKeyDown(keyCode: Int, event: KeyEvent): Boolean {
   207→        if (keyCode == KeyEvent.KEYCODE_F6 && event.action == KeyEvent.ACTION_DOWN) {
   208→            Log.d(TAG, "Hardware scan button (F6) pressed")
   209→            triggerScan()
   210→            return true
   211→        }
   212→        return false
   213→    }
   214→
   215→    /**
   216→     * Programmatically triggers a barcode scan by sending the nlscan.action.SCANNER_TRIG broadcast.
   217→     *
   218→     * This sends an intent to the Newland scanner service to initiate a barcode scan.
   219→     * The scan result will be received via the registered BroadcastReceiver listening
   220→     * for nlscan.action.SCANNER_RESULT and emitted through the scanEvents SharedFlow.
   221→     *
   222→     * This method can be called from ViewModels, UI components, or any other part of
   223→     * the application that needs to trigger a scan programmatically (e.g., via a button click).
   224→     *
   225→     * Example usage:
   226→     * ```
   227→     * // In a ViewModel or Activity
   228→     * scannerManager.triggerScan()
   229→     * ```
   230→     *
   231→     * Note: The BroadcastReceiver must be registered (via register()) before calling
   232→     * this method, otherwise scan results will not be received.
   233→

1→# Inventory APK
     2→
     3→An Android application for efficient inventory management and tracking.
     4→
     5→## 📋 Overview
     6→
     7→Inventory APK is a mobile inventory management solution designed to help businesses and individuals track, manage, and organize their inventory items efficiently. Built natively for Android, the application provides a streamlined interface for managing stock levels, tracking item movements, and generating reports on the go.
     8→
     9→## 🎯 Purpose
    10→
    11→Traditional inventory management often requires desktop software or complex web applications. Inventory APK brings the power of inventory tracking directly to your mobile device, enabling:
    12→
    13→- **Real-time tracking**: Update inventory status immediately as items move
    14→- **Offline-first operation**: Manage inventory without constant internet connectivity
    15→- **Mobile convenience**: Use your smartphone to scan, update, and view inventory anywhere
    16→- **Simplified workflow**: Intuitive interface designed for quick data entry and retrieval
    17→
    18→## ✨ Key Features
    19→
    20→### Core Functionality (Planned)
    21→- **Item Management**: Add, edit, and delete inventory items with detailed information
    22→- **Barcode Scanning**: Quick item lookup and data entry using device camera
    23→- **Stock Tracking**: Monitor quantity levels with low-stock alerts
    24→- **Category Organization**: Group items by custom categories and tags
    25→- **Search & Filter**: Quickly find items using powerful search capabilities
    26→- **Offline Support**: Full functionality without internet connection
    27→- **Data Export**: Export inventory data to CSV/Excel formats
    28→
    29→### Advanced Features (Future Roadmap)
    30→- **Multi-location Support**: Manage inventory across multiple warehouses or stores
    31→- **Supplier Management**: Track vendor information and purchase orders
    32→- **Stock Movement History**: Complete audit trail of inventory changes
    33→- **Reporting & Analytics**: Visual insights into inventory trends
    34→- **Cloud Sync**: Synchronize data across multiple devices
    35→- **Multi-user Access**: Collaborative inventory management with role-based permissions
    36→
    37→> **Note**: This project is in active development. See [ROADMAP.md](ROADMAP.md) for detailed feature timeline and MVP scope.
    38→
    39→## 🚀 Getting Started
    40→
    41→### Prerequisites
    42→
    43→Before you begin, ensure you have the following installed:
    44→
    45→- **Android Studio**: Arctic Fox (2020.3.1) or later
    46→- **JDK**: Java Development Kit 11 or higher
    47→- **Android SDK**: API Level 24 (Android 7.0) minimum
    48→- **Git**: For version control
    49→
    50→### Installation
    51→
    52→1. **Clone the repository**
    53→   ```bash
    54→   git clone https://github.com/yourusername/inventory-apk.git
    55→   cd inventory-apk
    56→   ```
    57→
    58→2. **Open in Android Studio**
    59→   - Launch Android Studio
    60→   - Select "Open an Existing Project"
    61→   - Navigate to the cloned repository folder
    62→   - Click "OK"
    63→
    64→3. **Sync Gradle dependencies**
    65→   - Android Studio will automatically prompt to sync
    66→   - Or manually sync via: File → Sync Project with Gradle Files
    67→
    68→4. **Configure Android Emulator** (optional)
    69→   - Open AVD Manager (Tools → AVD Manager)
    70→   - Create a new Virtual Device with API Level 24+
    71→   - Or connect a physical Android device with USB debugging enabled
    72→
    73→5. **Build and Run**
    74→   - Click the "Run" button (green play icon)
    75→   - Select your target device (emulator or physical device)
    76→   - The app will build and install automatically
    77→
    78→### Quick Start Guide
    79→
    80→Once the app is installed:
    81→
    82→1. **First Launch**: The app will create a local database for your inventory
    83→2. **Add Your First Item**:
    84→   - Tap the "+" floating action button
    85→   - Enter item details (name, SKU, quantity, etc.)
    86→   - Optionally scan a barcode for quick entry
    87→   - Save the item
    88→3. **View Inventory**: Browse your items in the main list view
    89→4. **Update Stock**: Tap any item to adjust quantity or edit details
    90→5. **Search Items**: Use the search bar to filter by name, SKU, or category
    91→

1→# Architecture Documentation
     2→
     3→## 📐 System Overview
     4→
     5→Inventory APK is a native Android application designed with a clean, modular architecture that prioritizes offline-first functionality, maintainability, and scalability. The application follows modern Android development best practices, leveraging the Android Jetpack suite to provide a robust, testable, and user-friendly inventory management solution.
     6→
     7→### High-Level Architecture
     8→
     9→```
    10→┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
    11→│                     Presentation Layer                   │
    12→│              (Jetpack Compose UI + ViewModels)          │
    13→└────────────────────┬────────────────────────────────────┘
    14→                     │
    15→                     ▼
    16→┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
    17→│                      Domain Layer                        │
    18→│              (Use Cases + Business Logic)                │
    19→└────────────────────┬────────────────────────────────────┘
    20→                     │
    21→                     ▼
    22→┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
    23→│                       Data Layer                         │
    24→│         (Repositories + Room Database + APIs)            │
    25→└─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
    26→```
    27→
    28→### Core Principles
    29→
    30→1. **Offline-First**: All core functionality works without internet connectivity
    31→2. **Separation of Concerns**: Clear boundaries between UI, business logic, and data
    32→3. **Single Source of Truth**: Room database as the authoritative data source
    33→4. **Unidirectional Data Flow**: Data flows from data layer → domain → presentation
    34→5. **Testability**: Architecture enables comprehensive unit and integration testing
    35→6. **Reactive Programming**: UI updates automatically when data changes
    36→
    37→---
    38→
    39→## 🏛️ MVVM Architecture Pattern
    40→
    41→### Overview
    42→
    43→Inventory APK implements the **Model-View-ViewModel (MVVM)** architecture pattern, which provides clear separation between business logic and UI code, facilitating maintainability and testability.
    44→
    45→### Component Breakdown
    46→
    47→#### **Model**
    48→- **Definition**: Represents the data layer and business logic
    49→- **Components**:
    50→  - Room database entities
    51→  - Repository implementations
    52→  - Domain models
    53→  - Data sources (local database, future API services)
    54→- **Responsibilities**:
    55→  - Data persistence and retrieval
    56→  - Business rule enforcement
    57→  - Data transformation and validation
    58→
    59→#### **View**
    60→- **Definition**: UI layer responsible for displaying data and capturing user input
    61→- **Components**:
    62→  - Jetpack Compose composables
    63→  - Activity/Fragment containers (minimal usage)
    64→  - UI state representations
    65→- **Responsibilities**:
    66→  - Render UI based on ViewModel state
    67→  - Capture user interactions
    68→  - Navigate between screens
    69→  - Display loading, error, and success states
    70→
    71→#### **ViewModel**
    72→- **Definition**: Mediator between View and Model
    73→- **Components**:
    74→  - AndroidX ViewModel classes
    75→  - UI state holders
    76→  - Use case orchestrators
    77→- **Responsibilities**:
    78→  - Expose UI state to the View via StateFlow/LiveData
    79→  - Handle user actions and delegate to Use Cases
    80→  - Manage UI-related data lifecycle
    81→  - Survive configuration changes
    82→  - No Android framework dependencies (except AndroidX ViewModel)
    83→
    84→### Data Flow Example
    85→
    86→```
    87→User Action (e.g., "Add Item")
    88→        │
    89→        ▼
    90→    Composable (View)
    91→        │
    92→        ▼
    93→    ViewModel.onAddItemClicked()
    94→        │
    95→        ▼
    96→    AddItemUseCase.execute()
    97→        │
    98→        ▼
    99→    ItemRepository.insertItem()
   100→        │

     3→  "description": "# Dependency Injection with Hilt

Set up Hilt as the DI framework across the application. Configure Application-scoped, Activity-scoped, and ViewModel-scoped components. Provide singleton instances for database, networking, scanner manager, and repository classes.

## Rationale
Hilt is specified in the tech stack (CLAUDE.md) and is essential for testability, modular architecture, and proper singleton lifecycle management. The NewlandScannerManager must be Application-scoped, and repositories need proper scoping for data layer isolation.

- As a developer, I want dependency injection so that components are loosely coupled and easily testable

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] Hilt configured with @HiltAndroidApp Application class
- [ ] At least one @Module providing Application-scoped dependencies
- [ ] ViewModels use @HiltViewModel annotation
- [ ] Activities annotated with @AndroidEntryPoint
- [ ] Dependency graph compiles without errors

     6→import com.inventory.scanner.NewlandScannerManager
     7→import com.inventory.scanner.ScanResult
     8→import dagger.hilt.android.lifecycle.HiltViewModel
     9→import kotlinx.coroutines.flow.MutableStateFlow
    10→import kotlinx.coroutines.flow.StateFlow
    11→import kotlinx.coroutines.flow.asStateFlow
    12→import kotlinx.coroutines.launch
    13→import javax.inject.Inject
    14→
    15→/**
    16→ * Main ViewModel for the Inventory application.
    17→ *
    18→ * Manages UI state and coordinates with the NewlandScannerManager to process
    19→ * barcode scans. This ViewModel is scoped to the Activity/Fragment lifecycle
    20→ * and survives configuration changes.
    21→ *
    22→ * Key features:
    23→ * - Collects scan events from NewlandScannerManager's SharedFlow
    24→ * - Exposes scan results to the UI via StateFlow
    25→ * - Handles scanner lifecycle in coordination with Activity lifecycle
    26→ *
    27→ * @property scannerManager Injected singleton NewlandScannerManager instance
    28→ */
    29→@HiltViewModel
    30→class MainViewModel @Inject constructor(
    31→    private val scannerManager: NewlandScannerManager
    32→) : ViewModel() {
    33→
    34→    companion object {
    35→        private const val TAG = "MainViewModel"
    36→    }
    37→
    38→    /**
    39→     * Internal mutable state for the last scanned barcode.
    40→     * Exposed as immutable StateFlow to the UI layer.
    41→     */
    42→    private val _lastScanResult = MutableStateFlow<ScanResult?>(null)
    43→
    44→    /**
    45→     * Public StateFlow for observing scan results in the UI.
    46→     *
    47→     * Collectors will receive updates whenever a new barcode is scanned.
    48→     * Initial value is null until the first scan is received.
    49→     *
    50→     * Example usage in Compose:
    51→     * ```
    52→     * val scanResult by viewModel.lastScanResult.collectAsState()
    53→     * scanResult?.let { result ->
    54→     *     Text("Scanned: ${result.barcode}")
    55→     * }
    56→     * ```
    57→     */
    58→    val lastScanResult: StateFlow<ScanResult?> = _lastScanResult.asStateFlow()
    59→
    60→    init {
    61→        // Start collecting scan events from the scanner manager
    62→        observeScanEvents()
    63→        Log.d(TAG, "MainViewModel initialized")
    64→    }
    65→
    66→    /**
    67→     * Observes scan events from NewlandScannerManager and updates UI state.
    68→     *
    69→     * This coroutine runs for the lifetime of the ViewModel (viewModelScope)
    70→     * and automatically cancels when the ViewModel is cleared.
    71→     */
    72→    private fun observeScanEvents() {
    73→        viewModelScope.launch {
    74→            scannerManager.scanEvents.collect { scanResult ->
    75→                Log.d(TAG, "Received scan: ${scanResult.barcode} (${scanResult.barcodeType})")
    76→                _lastScanResult.value = scanResult
    77→            }
    78→        }
    79→    }
    80→
    81→    /**
    82→     * Clears the last scan result from UI state.
    83→     *
    84→     * Call this when the user dismisses a scan result dialog or navigates away
    85→     * from the scan result screen.
    86→     */
    87→    fun clearScanResult() {
    88→        Log.d(TAG, "Clearing scan result")
    89→        _lastScanResult.value = null
    90→    }
    91→
    92→    override fun onCleared() {
    93→        super.onCleared()
    94→        Log.d(TAG, "MainViewModel cleared")
    95→    }
    96→}
    97→

1→package com.inventory.ui
     2→
     3→import android.util.Log
     4→import androidx.lifecycle.ViewModel
     5→import androidx.lifecycle.viewModelScope
     6→import com.inventory.scanner.NewlandScannerManager
     7→import com.inventory.scanner.ScanResult
     8→import dagger.hilt.android.lifecycle.HiltViewModel
     9→import kotlinx.coroutines.flow.MutableStateFlow
    10→import kotlinx.coroutines.flow.StateFlow
    11→import kotlinx.coroutines.flow.asStateFlow
    12→import kotlinx.coroutines.launch
    13→import javax.inject.Inject
    14→
    15→/**
    16→ * Main ViewModel for the Inventory application.
    17→ *
    18→ * Manages UI state and coordinates with the NewlandScannerManager to process
    19→ * barcode scans. This ViewModel is scoped to the Activity/Fragment lifecycle
    20→ * and survives configuration changes.
    21→ *
    22→ * Key features:
    23→ * - Collects scan events from NewlandScannerManager's SharedFlow
    24→ * - Exposes scan results to the UI via StateFlow
    25→ * - Handles scanner lifecycle in coordination with Activity lifecycle
    26→ *
    27→ * @property scannerManager Injected singleton NewlandScannerManager instance
    28→ */
    29→@HiltViewModel
    30→class MainViewModel @Inject constructor(
    31→    private val scannerManager: NewlandScannerManager
    32→) : ViewModel() {
    33→
    34→    companion object {
    35→        private const val TAG = "MainViewModel"
    36→    }
    37→
    38→    /**
    39→     * Internal mutable state for the last scanned barcode.
    40→     * Exposed as immutable StateFlow to the UI layer.
    41→     */
    42→    private val _lastScanResult = MutableStateFlow<ScanResult?>(null)
    43→
    44→    /**
    45→     * Public StateFlow for observing scan results in the UI.
    46→     *
    47→     * Collectors will receive updates whenever a new barcode is scanned.
    48→     * Initial value is null until the first scan is received.
    49→     *
    50→     * Example usage in Compose:
    51→     * ```
    52→     * val scanResult by viewModel.lastScanResult.collectAsState()
    53→     * scanResult?.let { result ->
    54→     *     Text("Scanned: ${result.barcode}")
    55→     * }
    56→     * ```
    57→     */
    58→    val lastScanResult: StateFlow<ScanResult?> = _lastScanResult.asStateFlow()
    59→
    60→    init {
    61→        // Start collecting scan events from the scanner manager
    62→        observeScanEvents()
    63→        Log.d(TAG, "MainViewModel initialized")
    64→    }
    65→
    66→    /**
    67→     * Observes scan events from NewlandScannerManager and updates UI state.
    68→     *
    69→     * This coroutine runs for the lifetime of the ViewModel (viewModelScope)
    70→     * and automatically cancels when the ViewModel is cleared.
    71→     */
    72→    private fun observeScanEvents() {
    73→        viewModelScope.launch {
    74→            scannerManager.scanEvents.collect { scanResult ->
    75→                Log.d(TAG, "Received scan: ${scanResult.barcode} (${scanResult.barcodeType})")
    76→                _lastScanResult.value = scanResult
    77→            }
    78→        }
    79→    }
    80→
    81→    /**
    82→     * Clears the last scan result from UI state.
    83→     *
    84→     * Call this when the user dismisses a scan result dialog or navigates away
    85→     * from the scan result screen.
    86→     */
    87→    fun clearScanResult() {
    88→        Log.d(TAG, "Clearing scan result")
    89→        _lastScanResult.value = null
    90→    }
    91→
    92→    override fun onCleared() {
    93→        super.onCleared()
    94→        Log.d(TAG, "MainViewModel cleared")
    95→    }
    96→}
    97→

1→package com.inventory.scanner
     2→
     3→import android.content.BroadcastReceiver
     4→import android.content.Context
     5→import android.content.Intent
     6→import android.util.Log
     7→import android.view.KeyEvent
     8→import dagger.hilt.android.qualifiers.ApplicationContext
     9→import kotlinx.coroutines.CoroutineScope
    10→import kotlinx.coroutines.Dispatchers
    11→import kotlinx.coroutines.SupervisorJob
    12→import kotlinx.coroutines.flow.MutableSharedFlow
    13→import kotlinx.coroutines.flow.SharedFlow
    14→import kotlinx.coroutines.flow.asSharedFlow
    15→import kotlinx.coroutines.launch
    16→import javax.inject.Inject
    17→import javax.inject.Singleton
    18→
    19→/**
    20→ * Singleton manager for the Newland MT90 barcode scanner.
    21→ *
    22→ * Handles lifecycle management of the scanner BroadcastReceiver, processes scan events,
    23→ * implements debouncing to prevent duplicate scans, and provides a reactive SharedFlow
    24→ * for consuming scan results in ViewModels and UI layers.
    25→ *
    26→ * Key features:
    27→ * - Registers/unregisters BroadcastReceiver for nlscan.action.SCANNER_RESULT
    28→ * - Handles hardware scan button (F6 key)
    29→ * - Supports programmatic scan triggering via nlscan.action.SCANNER_TRIG
    30→ * - 300ms debounce protection against double-scans
    31→ * - Reactive API via SharedFlow<ScanResult>
    32→ *
    33→ * @property context Application context injected by Hilt
    34→ */
    35→@Singleton
    36→class NewlandScannerManager @Inject constructor(
    37→    @ApplicationContext private val context: Context
    38→) {
    39→    companion object {
    40→        private const val TAG = "NewlandScannerManager"
    41→
    42→        // Newland MT90 scanner broadcast actions
    43→        private const val ACTION_SCANNER_RESULT = "nlscan.action.SCANNER_RESULT"
    44→        private const val ACTION_SCANNER_TRIG = "nlscan.action.SCANNER_TRIG"
    45→
    46→        // Intent extra keys for scan data (based on Newland MT90 SDK)
    47→        private const val EXTRA_BARCODE_DATA = "SCAN_BARCODE1"
    48→        private const val EXTRA_BARCODE_TYPE = "SCAN_BARCODE_TYPE"
    49→
    50→        // Debounce protection: ignore scans within 300ms of the previous scan
